characters.relation: Tyrant who courted him, then seized power
characters.blurb: Kinsman who flattered Solon’s reforms, staged a wound, and took a bodyguard—beginning the tyranny Solon alone opposed aloud.
Showed Solon his treasure and asked who was happiest; Solon’s “Call no man happy until dead” outlived Croesus’s fall to Cyrus.
One of the Seven Sages. Stories pair him with Solon on travel, politics, and the limits of private life.
Mocked Solon’s written laws—like spider-webs that catch the weak—yet lodged with him as a friend.
His homicide laws Solon kept; the rest of Draco’s death-penalties Solon repealed as written in blood.
Head of a great house; married his daughter to Pisistratus, then quarreled when the tyrant insulted her.
Founder-figure of the Alcmaeonidae, the clan tangled in Cylon’s curse and later in Pisistratus’s feuds.
Tried to seize the Acropolis; his killers’ sacrilege stained the Alcmaeonidae—a pollution Solon’s age still managed.
Modest citizen who lived well, saw children settled, and died gloriously in battle—Solon’s answer to Croesus.
With Biton he drew his mother’s ox-cart to Hera’s temple and died in his glory—Solon’s second example of happiness.
Shared Cleobis’s pious labor and blessed death; Herodotus’s tale Solon retells at Sardis.
Took Sardis and nearly burned Croesus; the Lydian’s cry of Solon’s name spared him in the famous story.
Ruler of Soli in Cyprus who took Solon’s advice on relocating his city; Solon praised him in verse.
When Thespis’s drama lied for sport, old Solon struck his staff and warned that play-lies would breed political ones.
Brought in to cleanse the city after pollution; his visit belongs to the generation of Solon’s reforms.
Named with the Seven Sages; his harsh rule is a foil to Solon’s refusal of tyranny.
Another of the Seven; like Solon he held power briefly to settle stasis, then laid it down.
Counted among the Seven Wise Men; appears in the traveling company of wisdom stories around Solon.

characters.relation: Fabulist Solon met at Croesus’s court
characters.blurb: In one anecdote Aesop advises Solon how to speak to kings—then suffers Croesus’s anger himself.
Solon’s father, of good birth but moderate means—the pedigree of a reformer who still worked in trade.
Academic writer Plutarch quotes on Solon’s laws, travels, and the Atlantis tale shared with Plato.

characters.relation: Preserves the Atlantis story from Solon
characters.blurb: Says Critias heard of Atlantis through Dropides from Solon—the Egyptian priests’ tale.
Solon’s cancellation of debts and debt-slavery—the centerpiece of his social peace, hated by some creditors.
Old homicide court and council of ex-archons; Solon kept it as a guardian of the laws.
Eresian writer Plutarch cites for anecdotes of Solon’s politics and private sayings.
Greek foil: Lycurgus could bind Sparta by oath and absence; Solon’s Athens rewrote his laws in his lifetime.

Athenian merchant-poet who cancelled debts, freed enslaved citizens, and gave Athens a written constitution—then left the city for ten years so no one could force him to repeal it. His verse survives as witness and self-defence.
Solon consulted Delphi before publishing his laws; Apollo's sanction gave the legislation a divine warranty to set beside its rational merit.
Solon is said to have arranged and recited Homer's poems in a fixed order at the Panathenaia—one of his cultural acts alongside lawgiving.
Plutarch draws on the Athenian Constitution attributed to Aristotle's school when reconstructing Solon's classes, courts, and the legal logic behind the Seisactheia.
Alexandrian writer who listed stories of the philosophers and sages; Plutarch borrows several of his anecdotes about Solon's travels and wit.
Aristotle's successor, cited by Plutarch for interpretations of specific Solonian statutes and their intention.

characters.relation: Elegist with whom Solon argued in verse
characters.blurb: Colophonian poet who set sixty as the happy limit of life; Solon wrote back in elegiac verse insisting eighty was a finer end—an argument conducted entirely in poetry.

characters.relation: Homeric hero in the Salamis dispute
characters.blurb: Solon inserted lines about Ajax into a Homeric recitation to prove that Athens—not Megara—had ancestral claim on Salamis; the hero's Attic grave-site sealed the argument.

characters.relation: Son of Ajax who settled Attica
characters.blurb: Ajax's son who chose Athens over Salamis and gave the Athenians their genealogical claim to the island that Solon used in the arbitration dispute with Megara.

characters.relation: Cousin who helped Solon recite on Salamis
characters.blurb: Solon's kinsman who accompanied him when he staged his mad recitation in the agora to rouse Athens for Salamis—one of the few named participants in the episode.
Brought formal charges against the Alcmaeonid family for the sacrilegious killing after Cylon's attempted coup—the pollution Solon's age still had to manage.

characters.relation: Poet whose song Solon wished to learn
characters.blurb: In the famous anecdote, Solon heard his nephew sing one of Sappho's songs at table and asked to be taught it—he said he wished to know it before he died.

characters.relation: Solon's kinsman; link to the Atlantis tale
characters.blurb: Solon's relative through whom the story of Atlantis, told him by Egyptian priests, eventually reached Critias and so Plato's dialogues.
